raw_case:
Unusual hand tumor: A case report
Adipose tumors are dominated by lipomas, but other rarer entities may be encountered such as hibernoma which is a benign tumor that develops from brown fat. The thigh is the preferred location for hibernomas, but the tumor can appear in other areas. The upper limb is a rare localization and there is only 1 case of hibernoma on the hand already reported in the English literature. We report the case of a hibernoma of the palmar aspect of the hand. The tumor had no clinical specificities. MRI showed an uncertain appearance mimicking liposarcoma. The diagnosis was only confirmed by histological study. The treatment consisted of complete excision of the tumor. At a follow-up of 8 months, there was no recurrence.

Introduction

Hibernoma is a rare benign tumor of brown fat. Since the first description by Merkel in 1906, less than 250 cases have been reported in the English literature. The tumor is often described as an asymptomatic mass of soft tissue, located in a proximal position, where the brown fat persists, thus the thigh is the preferential location for hibernomas. The localization in the hand is extremely rare and to our knowledge, just 1 case of a dorsal hibernoma of the thumb has been documented.Clinically, the tumor has no specific symptoms. In the MRI, differential diagnoses include lipoma and liposarcoma. The diagnosis is confirmed by histological study. Complete surgical excision is the mainstay of treatment.We report a case of Hibernoma of the thenar eminence.

Case report

The patient was a 61-year-old, without medical history, complaining of a subcutaneous tumor filling the palmar aspect of the first webspace of the left hand. The painless lump had appeared 5 years ago and gradually increased in size. The skin above was tight with normal coloration. The tumor was soft and fixed [Fig. 1A].The protrusion of the mass which measured 7 × 4 cm was limiting the opposition of the thumb. There was no neurological complaint [Fig. 1B].The MRI showed a subcutaneous limited fat-containing mass which measured 80 × 35 mm, crossed by vascular structures [Fig. 2, Fig. 3]. The tumor reached the carpal tunnel and surrounded the volar aspect of the median nerve and the flexor tendons of the second, third and fourth finger. The main differential diagnosis was liposarcoma on account of the size and the vascularization.We proceeded by a surgical biopsy. In histological examination, the tumor contained uniform large cells resembling brown fat intermixed with mature adipocytes.Two weeks later, the patient underwent resection of the mass under regional anesthesia. A 7 cm volar incision was required. The tumor was easily dissected and completely excised despite its close relation with the flexor tendon and the median nerve [Fig. 4]. Feeding vessels were ligated. After excision of the excess skin, the incision was closed.In histological examination, the mass was encapsulated and lobulated formed by brown fat and some mature adipocytes separated by fibrous septa, including vessels and inflammatory cells. There is no atypia [Fig. 5].There was no postoperative complication, and the patient retrieved a complete opposition of the thumb in few days. At 8-month-follow-up, there is no recurrence.

Discussion

Hibernomas are benign soft-tissue tumors emerging from vestigial brown fat, which can be located in the subcutaneous tissue, the skeletal muscle, or the intermuscular fascia [1].This tumor is uncommon and accounts for <2% of all adipocytic tumors [2].Brown fat is crucial in nonshivering thermogenesis in hibernating animals and the human fetus [3].The high concentration of mitochondria and hypervascularization leads to the dark color, which differentiates it from white adipose tissue [4].The largest and most valid case series of hibernomas was 170 patients by Furlong MA et al. [1], who proved that the common location is the thigh, followed by upper extremity, trunk, neck, then abdominal cavity or retroperitoneum.These tumors affect mainly adults in the 3rd and 4th decades of life with a slight male predominance [1,2,5].The clinical presentation may vary depending on the location. The tumor is often asymptomatic and present as soft, painless, palpable, and mobile masses. They become symptomatic only when associated with compression of surrounding structures due to their growth [1,6].Superficial hibernomas can be considered as lipomas, which are the same in presentation and much more common [4].The diagnosis of hibernoma is difficult. Radiographs may show a radiolucent mass, typically without calcification or bone erosion [7]. The ultrasound exam shows a consistently hyperechoic mass with hypervascularization and enlarged vessels [8].The MRI is the essential morphologic investigation, allowing a better characterization of the size and location of the tumor, and establishing the differential diagnosis with malignant tumors.Hibernomas are slightly or distinctly hypointense to subcutaneous fat on T1W images and don't fully suppress on STIR or fat-saturated T2W images [8,9].The differential diagnosis of a complex lipomatous tumor on MRI is wide and involves benign conditions, such as lipoma, angiolipoma, hemangioma, and malignant tumors such as liposarcoma.Poorly differentiated (high-grade) liposarcomas are rarely confused with lipomas, or hibernomas, as they include slight or no fatty components. Otherwise, the fatty component of a well-differentiated liposarcoma appears isointense to subcutaneous fat, on T1WSE, distinguishing them from hibernomas [8].The definitive diagnosis of Hibernoma is based on pathology. Grossly, the tumor is well-encapsulated, containing white and brown fat separated by fibrous septa and vessels. Its color may range from yellow to brown. On the microscopic examination, there are 3 principal cell types: cells with granular eosinophilic cytoplasm containing few lipid vacuoles; larger cells with multiples vacuoles; and univacuolated large cells. Generally, there is no mitosis or atypia [4].Histologically, the main differential diagnosis is the atypical lipomatous tumor, which is eliminated by negative MDM2 and CDK4 on immunohistochemistry [5].The surgical treatment consists of a monoblock excision of the tumor which must be ideally done with minimal dissection. The resected hibernoma is usually a smooth, well-encapsulated, and nonadherent mass.This excision is curative and has a low risk for local recurrence. When the diagnosis is uncertain, accurate hemostasis should be achieved and the patient should receive further evaluation and possibly treatment before definitive surgical management such as our case.The diagnosis is confirmed by histopathological examination of the resected tumor.Incomplete excision may occur in continued growth and recurrence, even in tumors that appear to be histologically benign.In all published reports of hibernomas in humans in the English literature, no cases have been reported of malignant transformation or distant metastasis.However, 2 cases of recurrence are reported, because of incomplete excision [10].

Patient consent

The author confirms that the patient has been fully informed about the nature of the procedure and has provided written consent.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 61-year-old, no previous medical history.
- **Chief Complaint**: Gradually enlarging subcutaneous tumor in the palmar aspect of the left hand, limiting thumb opposition.
- **History of Present Illness**: Painless lump present for 5 years with progressive size increase.
- **Past Medical History**: None.
- **Personal History**: None.
- **Family History**: None.
- **Physical Examination**: 7 × 4 cm tumor located in the palmar aspect of the first webspace of the left hand. Soft, fixed, skin above was tight with normal coloration, no neurological deficits.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - **MRI**: 80 × 35 mm fat-containing mass with vascular structures, extending to the carpal tunnel, surrounding flexor tendons and median nerve. Differential initially included liposarcoma.
  - **Histology**: Tumor consists of uniform large cells resembling brown fat intermixed with mature adipocytes, encapsulated, no atypia.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial List**: Based on painless soft tissue mass in the hand, differential diagnoses included lipoma, liposarcoma, angiolipoma, hemangioma, and hibernoma.
- **Step 1**: Rule out liposarcoma given the MRI findings of a vascularized fat-containing tumor without signs of aggressive malignancy (e.g., significant atypia, necrosis, or invasion).
- **Step 2**: Rule out lipoma due to the vascular features seen on MRI and histological findings of brown fat, which is not typical for lipoma.
- **Step 3**: Consider and confirm hibernoma based on MRI features (hypointensity relative to fat, partial suppression on fat-saturated images), consistent histological findings (brown fat intermixed with mature adipocytes, encapsulation, absent atypia or mitosis), and absence of markers for malignancy (e.g., negative MDM2 and CDK4, excluding atypical lipomatous tumor).
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Hibernoma of the palmar aspect of the left hand (thenar eminence).

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Initial assessment based on patient demographics and chief complaint: A 61-year-old patient presents with a gradually enlarging, painless subcutaneous tumor in the left hand for 5 years. The slow growth and painless nature suggest a benign or low-grade tumor, but the size and location are important factors to consider. The limitation of thumb opposition indicates potential functional impact and proximity to important anatomical structures.

<step 2> Evaluation of physical examination findings: The tumor is described as 7 × 4 cm, soft, fixed, located in the palmar first webspace, with tight skin but normal coloration and no neurological deficits noted clinically. The size is significant, and "soft" may suggest a lipomatous or cystic lesion. "Fixed" needs to be considered in relation to surrounding tissues. The absence of neurological deficits in physical exam is noted, but MRI indicates involvement of median nerve.

<step 3> Analysis of MRI findings: The MRI reveals a larger (80 × 35 mm) fat-containing mass with vascular structures, extending to the carpal tunnel and surrounding flexor tendons and median nerve.  The "fat-containing" nature strongly suggests a lipomatous tumor.  The extension to the carpal tunnel and involvement of surrounding structures raises concern for potential compression and influences surgical planning. The initial differential including liposarcoma is understandable given the size and location and potential for fat-containing malignancy.

<step 4>  Crucial interpretation of Histology results: The histology report describes a tumor composed of uniform large cells resembling brown fat intermixed with mature adipocytes, encapsulated, and without atypia. This histological description is highly specific. The presence of brown fat-like cells and mature adipocytes points towards tumors derived from adipose tissue. The "encapsulated" nature and "no atypia" are critical in excluding malignant tumors like liposarcoma, which typically shows atypia and is often not well-encapsulated.

<step 5> Synthesizing findings to reach a diagnosis: Combining the MRI finding of a fat-containing mass with the definitive histology of brown fat-like cells and mature adipocytes without atypia, the most likely diagnosis is a **Hibernoma**. Hibernomas are benign tumors of brown fat.  While liposarcoma was considered initially due to MRI and size, the histology definitively rules it out. The slow growth, painless nature, and location are also consistent with Hibernoma, although the hand is not the most typical location.

<step 6> Final diagnostic confirmation and conclusion: The histological findings are conclusive for a benign brown fat tumor.  All other clinical and radiological findings are compatible with this diagnosis. Therefore, the final diagnosis is Hibernoma.

### Answer:
Hibernoma
